From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Hit record](/w/index.php?title=Hit_record&redirect=no "Hit record"))

Recorded song that becomes popular or commercially successful

"Hit record" redirects here. For the song by Custard, see [Hit Song](/wiki/Hit_Song "Hit Song"). For the online collaborative production company, see [HitRecord](/wiki/HitRecord "HitRecord").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Hit_song "Special:EditPage/Hit song") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Hit song"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Hit+song%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Hit+song%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Hit+song%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Hit+song%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Hit+song%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Hit+song%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(June 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **hit song**, also known as a **hit record**, **hit single**, or simply **hit**, is a [recorded](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording") [song](/wiki/Song "Song") or [instrumental](/wiki/Instrumental "Instrumental") that becomes broadly popular or well-known. Although *hit song* means any widely played or big-selling song, the specific term *hit record* usually refers to a [single](/wiki/Single_(music) "Single (music)") that has appeared in an official [music chart](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart") through repeated [radio airplay](/wiki/Airplay_(song) "Airplay (song)") audience impressions or significant streaming data and commercial sales.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Prior to the dominance of recorded music, commercial [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music") sales of individual songs were similarly promoted and tracked as singles and [albums](/wiki/Album "Album") are now. For example, in 1894, Edward B. Marks and Joe Stern released *[The Little Lost Child](/wiki/The_Little_Lost_Child "The Little Lost Child")*, which sold more than a million copies nationwide, based mainly on its success as an [illustrated song](/wiki/Illustrated_song "Illustrated song"), analogous to what later became [music videos](/wiki/Music_video "Music video").

## Chart hits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hit_song&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Chart hits")]

In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), a single is usually considered a hit when it reaches the top 40 of the [*Billboard* Hot 100](/wiki/Billboard_Hot_100 "Billboard Hot 100") or the top 75 of the [UK Singles Chart](/wiki/UK_Singles_Chart "UK Singles Chart"). The *[Guinness Book of British Hit Singles](/wiki/Guinness_Book_of_British_Hit_Singles "Guinness Book of British Hit Singles")* has used this definition since the 1970s. Some argue that reaching at least the top 100—since the [Official Charts Company](/wiki/Official_Charts_Company "Official Charts Company") increased the chart size on their website on 23 June 2007—lets a single be considered a hit due to the increasing singles market after chart rules included download singles. In most cases, the amount of weeks spent on either music chart may also play a big part in its relevance as a 'hit', regardless of its peak chart position.

A hit single is variously called a *number one hit*, a *top 5 hit*, a *top 10 hit*, a *top 20 hit*, or a *top 40 hit*, depending on its peak position. In the UK (where radio play is not included in the official charts), this does not completely reflect the song's popularity—as the weekly chart position is based solely on direct comparison with concurrent sales of other singles. It is, therefore, not uncommon that a single fails to chart, but has actually sold more copies than other singles regarded as "hits" based on their higher chart placement in a period of low sales. (This is also possible in the United States—or anywhere—with slow but steady sellers; a number of minor hits, especially those that are popular in specific genre, have earned gold certifications despite relatively poor pop chart performances.)

## Sales figures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hit_song&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sales figures")]

In the UK, the number of sales required to achieve a hit single steadily declined in line with a general decline in single sales until the early 2000s. The number has, however, recovered strongly with growth in official [digital downloads](/wiki/Music_download "Music download"). 2011 was a record year for UK singles sales.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Actual figures vary considerably depending on the time of year. In 2010 a number one single usually sold around 100,000 copies per week; sales of around 30,000 were often sufficient to reach the top 10, and a single selling over 6,000 copies could make the top 40.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Albums of hit singles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hit_song&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Albums of hit singles")]

Record companies often release collections of hit singles by various artists as [compilation albums](/wiki/Compilation_album "Compilation album"), such as the *[Now That's What I Call Music!](/wiki/Now_That%27s_What_I_Call_Music! "Now That's What I Call Music!")* series. Well-known bands and artists also frequently release collections of their most popular singles as *[Greatest hits](/wiki/Greatest_hits "Greatest hits")* albums.

## See also

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hit_song&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: See also")]

* [List of record charts](/wiki/List_of_record_charts "List of record charts")
* [Record chart](/wiki/Record_chart "Record chart")
* [Sleeper hit](/wiki/Sleeper_hit "Sleeper hit")
